definition: Inspiring awe or reverence; worthy of profound respect; majestic.
confidence_reason: Attested in Old English religious texts with positive connotation of divine majesty.

definition: Causing great fear or dread; terrifying; dreadful.
confidence_reason: Documented in Middle English with strong negative emotional force.
usage_register: Shift from positive awe to fearful dread.

definition: Very bad or unpleasant; of poor quality.
usage_region: Semantic bleaching — loss of original intensity.

confidence_reason: Derived from Latin nescius (ignorant). Earliest English sense is pejorative.
usage_register: Origin: Latin nescius via Old French nice (foolish).

confidence: Intermediate stage between foolish and pleasant; used in technical and scholarly contexts.
Now the dominant sense in most registers.
usage_region: One of the most documented cases of amelioration in English.

definition: To come before; to precede; to anticipate.
confidence: From Latin praevenire (to come before). The original Latin sense was neutral — priority not obstruction.
usage_region: Calque of Latin praevenire. Now an opaque etymological sense.

definition: To hinder or obstruct; to keep from happening.
confidence: The semantic shift from preceding to obstructing is fully complete by 1625.

definition: To stop something from happening; to avert.
To carry; to bear (a burden or load).
confidence: Latin portare entered English through Old French during Norman borrowing.
usage_region: Root of import export transport portable: all trace to Latin portare.

confidence: From naught (nothing) + -y. Original sense was literal poverty not moral failing.
usage_region: Striking pejoration: poverty to moral badness.

confidence: The moral sense displaces the poverty sense entirely by 1520.
Further bleaching: from wicked to merely mischievous.
usage_region: Classic three-stage drift: poverty to evil to mischievous.

confidence: From Old English saelig (blessed). Cognate with German selig (blessed).

definition: Innocent; helpless; pitiable; deserving of compassion.
confidence: Shift from blessed to innocent — still positive or neutral.
Connotation turns negative — helplessness becomes foolishness.

usage_region: Complete journey from blessed to foolish in ~500 years.
